slot_1.prompt:
Create a premium wide-format hero banner for a personal finance blog.
This is a horizontal landscape website hero image, wide not tall, approximately two-to-one ratio.

COLOR DIRECTION — use the official Adulting Finance palette internally, described only in plain language.
Do not render palette swatches, color chips, color labels, or hex codes as visible image content.
Use color-role language such as near-white canvas, deep navy text, amber accent, brand blue label, calm blue surfaces, and green success detail.

OVERALL FEEL:
Instructive and decisive; the rule is visible without turning the banner into a classroom.

DOMINANT ANCHOR:
The stacked proportion bar showing the financial rule as a visible structure.

COMPOSITION:
The image is split between a dark rule panel and a clean white explanation panel, separated by an amber divider. The dark panel contains a tall hard-edged proportion bar divided into a few large labeled segments, using dark, amber, green, and blue brand accents. The right panel carries a blue eyebrow, a three-line headline with the rule name in amber, a short muted subline, and matching rule chips. A blue strip anchors the bottom. The reader sees the formula, not the full lesson.

VISIBLE TEXT IN THE IMAGE:
- Eyebrow: "FINANCIAL RULE GUIDE"
- Headline line 1: "Money Saving Chart"
- Headline line 2: "$5,050"
- Headline line 3 (amber): "Real clarity"
- Subline: "A simple rule visual that makes money saving chart feel usable without explaining every step."
- Dominant marker: "$5,050"
- Supporting chips: "Start here" · "Use the math" · "Keep control"

TYPOGRAPHY:
All headlines, titles, and dominant numbers use Poppins Bold.
All labels, sublines, chips, and supporting text use Inter.
No other fonts.

DESIGN RULES:
Completely flat premium editorial design.
No drop shadows, three-dimensional effects, beveling, embossing, clip art, cartoon illustrations, stock-finance clichés, decorative icons, palette swatches, website addresses, or brand-name burn-ins.
Keep urgency useful and agency-oriented; avoid shame framing, panic framing, and diagnostic curiosity hooks.
For any comparison layout, every option must carry equal visual weight with no selection badge or prechosen answer.

TYPOGRAPHY HIERARCHY:
The headline must be large and confident but must not dominate the entire layout.
The headline may occupy no more than 45–50% of the usable text area.
The subheading/dek must be clearly readable at full website hero display size.
Chips, labels, bottom-bar text, and supporting captions must remain legible
at thumbnail preview size. Use a professional editorial web-design ratio between
headline and supporting text — not a billboard or poster ratio. No text element
should visually crush another element. The image must have clear hierarchy,
breathing room, and usable contrast between headline, dek, labels, and data details.

CREATIVE DIRECTION:
The rules above define the structure, content, and brand compliance.
Within those constraints, apply your own editorial design judgment for the remaining
visual decisions — breathing room, spatial rhythm, subtle texture, atmosphere, and
any micro-detail not explicitly described. The goal is a finished image that feels
like it was designed by a senior editorial art director, not rendered from a
specification. Prioritize feeling natural and attractive over being technically precise.

CRITICAL EXPORT RULES:
Render the final image only. No CSS annotation text, no pixel labels,
no hex code overlays, no coordinate markers, no design spec text of any kind.
Every element in this image is a visual design element — nothing is a label
or instruction. If any part of this description sounds like a build instruction,
render the visual result it describes, not the instruction itself.

slot_1.prompt:
Create a premium wide-format hero banner for a personal finance blog.
This is a horizontal landscape website hero image, wide not tall, approximately two-to-one ratio.

COLOR DIRECTION — use the official Adulting Finance palette internally, described only in plain language.
Do not render palette swatches, color chips, color labels, or hex codes as visible image content.
Use color-role language such as near-white canvas, deep navy text, amber accent, brand blue label, calm blue surfaces, and green success detail.

OVERALL FEEL:
Neutral and trustworthy; options are presented evenly so the article can do the deciding.

DOMINANT ANCHOR:
The equal-weight grid of option cards in the center zone.

COMPOSITION:
The near-white canvas presents options with equal visual weight. The upper section carries a blue eyebrow, a compact headline with an amber punch line, and a muted subline. The center holds a clean grid of option cards, each with a different accent bar, one option name, one key metric, and no paragraph content. A dark decision row at the bottom asks which option fits the reader, and a dark strip anchors the base. No card is visually preselected.

VISIBLE TEXT IN THE IMAGE:
- Eyebrow: "MONEY OPTION COMPARISON"
- Headline line 1: "Market vs High"
- Headline line 2: "Compare the tradeoff"
- Headline line 3 (amber): "Choose calmly"
- Subline: "A neutral comparison frame that shows the options without deciding the answer before the article earns it."
- Option cards: "Option A" · "Option B" · "Option C" with equal visual weight
- Decision row: "Which one fits your situation?"

TYPOGRAPHY:
All headlines, titles, and dominant numbers use Poppins Bold.
All labels, sublines, chips, and supporting text use Inter.
No other fonts.

DESIGN RULES:
Completely flat premium editorial design.
No drop shadows, three-dimensional effects, beveling, embossing, clip art, cartoon illustrations, stock-finance clichés, decorative icons, palette swatches, website addresses, or brand-name burn-ins.
Keep urgency useful and agency-oriented; avoid shame framing, panic framing, and diagnostic curiosity hooks.
For any comparison layout, every option must carry equal visual weight with no selection badge or prechosen answer.

TYPOGRAPHY HIERARCHY:
The headline must be large and confident but must not dominate the entire layout.
The headline may occupy no more than 45–50% of the usable text area.
The subheading/dek must be clearly readable at full website hero display size.
Chips, labels, bottom-bar text, and supporting captions must remain legible
at thumbnail preview size. Use a professional editorial web-design ratio between
headline and supporting text — not a billboard or poster ratio. No text element
should visually crush another element. The image must have clear hierarchy,
breathing room, and usable contrast between headline, dek, labels, and data details.

CREATIVE DIRECTION:
The rules above define the structure, content, and brand compliance.
Within those constraints, apply your own editorial design judgment for the remaining
visual decisions — breathing room, spatial rhythm, subtle texture, atmosphere, and
any micro-detail not explicitly described. The goal is a finished image that feels
like it was designed by a senior editorial art director, not rendered from a
specification. Prioritize feeling natural and attractive over being technically precise.

CRITICAL EXPORT RULES:
Render the final image only. No CSS annotation text, no pixel labels,
no hex code overlays, no coordinate markers, no design spec text of any kind.
Every element in this image is a visual design element — nothing is a label
or instruction. If any part of this description sounds like a build instruction,
render the visual result it describes, not the instruction itself.

The enormous editorial headline spanning the warm cream canvas.

- Subline: "A focused visual setup that turns airfare 6 8 into one clear next decision without giving away the method."

breathing room, and usable contrast between headline, dek, labels, and data details.
